Cathy set the task then allowed the students to respond. She gave them time- one minute?- to begin their exploration. Two different answers came up. On bringing the class together she asked for an explanation of the two answers. The reasons given were attempts to uncover a rule. At this stage, she stopped them told them that before the end of the class they would have a rule and altered the task to can you make sense of the question/solution. The atmosphere in the class accepted this, there were no visual signs of frustration. Cathy used the language of \yes, but does it make sense?\" throughout the discussion, never hinting at 'the correctness' of each of the three methods shared. She expected the class to criticise the speaker introducing the roles, asking each to be friendly or sceptical. The 10 minutes were spent reinforcing the mathematical methods of representing problems in different ways, supporting your answers with reasons, analysing other ideas,checking methods through inverse operations; the learning process of re-explaining another point of view and the social learning of collaboration. Only after these processes had been explored was a traditional method introduced."

I agree, that our society has a genetic view of math ability. You're born with it or you aren't. Other cultures seem to have more of an effort or persistence view. \If you work hard you can get it.\" We have this view of general intelligence, too. I found that 7th grade was where many students who had been TAG finally hit the wall. What it meant to be good at math was no longer just memorize stuff. I used to have tearful conferences with TAG students and their parents, telling them that everyone hits the wall somewhere, and this is as good a time as any to re-set what they think being smart means. Being smart means that you can handle some frustration, can persevere, can get clear about where you are stuck, and are willing to ask question and listen to others."

I suppose the reason for that are comparative advantages of workers. The first to leave producing computers (in Mr. Professor's example) are those, who are more efficient at producing movies. So when they move, there is a significant increase in movies production, yet only a little loss in computer industry. The next workers who would leave are those with a little comparative advantage, and so on. So the last to choose working on movies instead of computers are those, who are more effective at producing computers. Their labour gives the smallest increase in movie production compared to the forgone production of computers.I believe this is the mainspring.

Girls and students of color have to overcome the stereotypes which they have been exposed to over and over; they are not as good at math at others. When interventions which are put into place which focus on the successes of what they have done and learned they are getting the reinforcement they need to overcome the negativity. When they are exposed to the fixed mindset which is focused on how smart they may be in mathematics, any negative feedback sets them further back and tends to affirm the stereotypes which they have been exposed to.High achieving girls are putting a great deal of pressure on themselves to please others. It is a common belief that girls are pleasers. They do what they can to please others. One of the reasons girls tend to give over-the-top projects to teachers. They go above and beyond to please the teacher. Whether this is true or not, I am not sure. However, it does appear to align with the high achieving girls who develop a fixed mindset. If they fail, then they have failed to please another.

\predict\" is what is known as a \"generic function\" in R, which means that it dispatches different methods depending upon the class of the object that is passed to the function.For example, if you pass a linear model to the predict function, it dispatches the method predict.lm to actually perform the prediction. Or if you pass a logistic regression model to the predict function, it dispatches the method predict.glm.If you run the following line of code in R, it will show you all of the methods for predict: methods(predict)And then once you know the method's name, you can get help on that method.If the line of code above does not work for a particular function, you can also try: showMethods(\"insert_function_name_here\")The methods() call works for what are called \"S3 functions\", whereas the showMethods() call works for \"S4 functions\".-<nameRedac_<anon_screen_name_redacted>>"

It wouldn't change what I do personally because I tutor one-to-one and treat every student as an individual. I agree that every student has endless potential with the right set of circumstances, however, everybody learns at a different pace and there are many learning styles. I am concerned that the tone of this video is about challenging students rather than stretching them. There is a significant \confidence\" element to maths learning, and pitching teaching too high and too fast for some students can crush that. Most students who come to me have given up at school because they feel too challenged and lessons move from topic to topic too quickly. I still strongly believe we should stream students by ability but maybe we should call it something else, like streaming for pace of learning or learning style. A good school will have flexibility to move students between classes as their speed of their development changes.At this point in the course, I don't feel that there is enough evidence presented on the speed of brain growth and ability to change what I do. There are plenty of examples of people with even mild brain injury who do not recover their abilities.Having read some of the posts here though, I have a few students who lack self-belief so I will share this research about brain growth with them."

Thanks a lot for your comments. I am still not fully convinced about the so called best answer: 'Risk is decreased by <zipRedac>5 women per <zipRedac>0,000 per year' . A woman who comes in and hears that info will freak out. That says basically only <zipRedac>5 out of <zipRedac>0000 per year will have lower risks of developing a breast cancer. The question she is going to ask am I going to be among those <zipRedac>5 or not ? Is this a lot ? Am I going to develop cancer? What's happening to woman of my age? To me, it sounds more informative to describe to a woman that she is potentially a person at risk (add some numbers, the risk is X person/ Y people) meaning she may or may not develop a cancer, BUT if she takes that drug, her risk of developing cancer will be halved. This is more concrete at the individual level. The key info is the risk reduction by 50% not some numbers people cannot refer to. This is my opinion. Any other takers? Profs / instructors ? Thanks.

What is original in the book is not true; and what is true is not original. In fact, as we shall find, even much that is fallacious in the book is not original, but can be found in a score of previous writers.\"Source: [Book PDF][1]Now look at this apparent paradox:\"Although natural disasters cause a great deal of damage disasters are also more likely to promote growth rather than retard it (Skidmore og Toya, <zipRedac>00<zipRedac>; The cost Japan, <zipRedac>011; Bill Emmott, <zipRedac>011; Cookson og Pilling <zipRedac>01 1; Horwich, <zipRedac>000). Similar evidence is found in the aftermath of the Kobe earthquake of 1995 in Japan. The earthquake, that struck in January 1995, resulted in more growth for every quarter, except the first, than in the previous year with a total GDP growth of 1.4% from a 0.6% growth in 1994 (Horwich, <zipRedac>000). The trend shows that unless there is a fundamental underlying weakness in the system or if there is a radical revolution following the disaster a natural disaster is likely to promote a short-term growth spurt (McRae,<zipRedac>007; Japan and growth, <zipRedac>011).\"[Source][<zipRedac>]Do natural disasters stimulate economic growth?: [NY Times Article][3]The Myth of U.S. Prosperity during World War II: [Independent Article][4]\"The strongest case for it has long been World War II, which Davis and Jaffe claim 'clearly was a boon for the U.S. economy.'
